title: Share of land defined as natural habitat
subtitle: Natural ecosystems include forest, grassland, savanna, and shrublands, and other classes such as peatlands and marshes. It is total land minus land that is used by humans for agriculture – either croplands or pasture – or for infrastructure and urban environments.
caption:
Share of land defined as natural habitat
Natural ecosystems include forest, grassland, savanna, and shrublands, and other classes such as peatlands and marshes. It is total land minus land that is used by humans for agriculture – either croplands or pasture – or for infrastructure and urban environments.

This line chart tracks the percentage of total land area classified as natural habitat across four distinct series—the World average, Germany, France, and the United Kingdom—spanning the years 1780 to 2010. The vertical axis measures the share of land from 0% to 80%, while the horizontal axis marks time in 20-year intervals. In 1780, the World average sits at the highest point on the chart, roughly 75%, while the three European nations cluster lower, between 45% and 65%. Over the next 170 years, all four lines show a downward trajectory. The global line descends steadily to a low point near 48% around 2000. The United Kingdom line drops from roughly 64% in 1780 to a minimum below 30% around 1950. Germany and France follow similar paths, with Germany falling to roughly 40% and France dipping to roughly 45% by the mid-20th century. A visible shift occurs after 1950 for the European countries. The lines for Germany, France, and the United Kingdom turn upward. By 2010, the Germany line reaches roughly 65%, the France line reaches roughly 62%, and the United Kingdom line recovers to roughly 48%. The World line remains relatively flat in the final decades, hovering just under 50%. The data shows a long-term decline in natural habitat share followed by a partial recovery in specific European nations during the late 20th century.
